title: Paternity and Adoption Leave Regulations 2002
chapters: SI 2002/2788 (Paternity) · SI 2002/2822 (Adoption) · Made under ERA 1996 ss.75A–80 · Amended by Paternity and Adoption Leave (Amendment) Regulations 2014 (SI 2014/2112) · ERA 2025 makes paternity leave a day-one right from 6 April 2026
desc: The Paternity and Adoption Leave Regulations 2002 provide the statutory framework for paternity leave and statutory adoption leave. Ordinary paternity leave (OPL) is 1 or 2 consecutive weeks, paid at the statutory rate (£194.32/week from April 2026) or 90% of average weekly earnings if lower. The ERA 2025 removes the 26-week qualifying period for paternity leave from 6 April 2026, making it a day-one right. The ERA 2025 also removes the restriction on taking paternity leave after shared parental leave — parents can now take paternity leave at any point within 52 weeks of birth/placement. Statutory adoption leave mirrors maternity leave: 52 weeks (26 weeks ordinary + 26 weeks additional), with SAP payable for 39 weeks. The qualifying period for adoption leave (26 weeks' continuous employment) was removed by SI 2014/2112. Bereaved Partners' Paternity Leave (ERA 2025 s.55) provides up to 52 weeks' leave if the mother or primary adopter dies within the first year.

parts.title: Paternity Leave and Statutory Paternity Pay

sections.title: Entitlement to paternity leave, duration, and notification
sections.summary: An employee is entitled to 1 or 2 consecutive weeks of ordinary paternity leave if they have (from 6 April 2026 — no qualifying period; previously 26 weeks' continuous service), are the father of the child or the mother's partner, and have responsibility for the child's upbringing. The employee must give notice of the expected week of childbirth and the chosen leave start date at least 15 weeks before the expected week of childbirth. Leave must be taken within 52 weeks of birth/placement (ERA 2025 removes the previous 56-day window and restriction on taking after SPL). Statutory Paternity Pay is payable at the prescribed rate (£194.32/week from April 2026) or 90% of average weekly earnings, whichever is lower.
sections.text:
Entitlement to ordinary paternity leave:

• Duration: 1 or 2 consecutive weeks (employee's choice)
• Qualifying period: none from 6 April 2026 (ERA 2025 s.14 removes the previous 26-week requirement)
• Eligibility: the employee must be the father of the child, or the spouse/civil partner/partner of the mother, and have or expect to have responsibility for the child's upbringing
• Timing: must be taken within 52 weeks of birth or placement for adoption
• ERA 2025: paternity leave may now be taken after shared parental leave (previous restriction removed)
• Pay: Statutory Paternity Pay at the prescribed weekly rate or 90% of average weekly earnings, whichever is lower
• Notification: at least 15 weeks before the expected week of childbirth (or within 7 days of notification of matching for adoption)
• Protection: dismissal or detriment for taking or seeking to take paternity leave is automatically unfair (no qualifying period for unfair dismissal claim)

versions.description: Original Paternity and Adoption Leave Regulations 2002 came into force. Introduced ordinary paternity leave (1 or 2 weeks). Required 26 weeks' continuous employment.

Shared Parental Leave Regulations 2014 created the option for parents to share up to 50 weeks of leave and 37 weeks of pay. Additional paternity leave (2–26 weeks) was abolished and replaced by shared parental leave.

ERA 2025 s.14: paternity leave becomes a day-one right. s.16–17: restriction on taking paternity leave after shared parental leave removed. Employees who will become entitled on 6 April 2026 could give notice from 18 February 2026.

leadingCases.name: Shuter v Ford Motor Company Ltd [2006] ICR 697
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT considered the interaction between paternity leave and the employer's shift patterns. The tribunal held that an employee on paternity leave is entitled to return to the same job with the same terms and conditions, including the same shift pattern.
leadingCases.significance: Early authority on the right to return from paternity leave to the same job.

leadingCases.name: Capita Customer Management Ltd v Ali [2019] EWCA Civ 900
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that paying enhanced maternity pay but only statutory paternity/shared parental pay to fathers does not constitute direct sex discrimination. Maternity leave serves a different purpose (health recovery, breastfeeding) from paternity/SPL (childcare).
leadingCases.significance: Definitively resolved the enhanced maternity pay vs paternity pay discrimination question. Employers can lawfully pay enhanced maternity pay without matching it for paternity or SPL.

refs: ERA 2025 s.55 (Bereaved Partners' Paternity Leave — up to 52 weeks)
Social Security Contributions and Benefits Act 1992 ss.171ZA–171ZEE (Statutory Paternity Pay)

pendingAmendments: ERA 2025 s.14 — paternity leave day-one right IN FORCE 6 April 2026
ERA 2025 ss.16-17 — paternity leave after SPL restriction removed
ERA 2025 s.55 — Bereaved Partners' Paternity Leave (up to 52 weeks) — not yet in force

leadingCases.name: Capita Customer Management v Ali [2019] EWCA Civ 900
leadingCases.heldText: Enhanced maternity pay without matching paternity/SPL pay is not direct sex discrimination.
leadingCases.significance: Key authority — employers can offer enhanced maternity pay without matching for fathers.

leadingCases.name: Shuter v Ford Motor Company [2006] ICR 697
leadingCases.heldText: Employee on paternity leave entitled to return to same job with same terms.
leadingCases.significance: Right to return from paternity leave.

parliamentaryOrigin.enablingAct: Employment Rights Act 1996 ss.75A–80 (as inserted by Employment Act 2002)
parliamentaryOrigin.madeBy: Secretary of State for Trade and Industry
parliamentaryOrigin.note: The PAL 2002 Regulations were made under the enabling provisions inserted into ERA 1996 by the Employment Act 2002. Paternity leave was a key Labour government family-friendly policy. ERA 2025 makes paternity leave a day-one right from 6 April 2026.
